meta.title: 6.1 Observability for AI-Powered Platforms
meta.description: Understand why infrastructure observability is insufficient for an AI platform — a healthy server can host a degraded model — and learn the three-layer stack: infrastructure, application services, and AI-specific signals like agent completion rate and first-turn success rate.

blocks.prompt:
Traditional observability tells you "the server is slow" or "this API is returning 500s." What's different about observability when the "business logic" is a probabilistic AI model that can silently degrade without throwing errors?

*Write your answer before reading. This primes your brain to notice what confirms or challenges your assumption.*

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you the mental model for why AI platforms require a third observability layer beyond infrastructure and application monitoring. By the end, you'll be able to explain "silent degradation" to any engineer, name the proxy metrics that detect it, and identify which observability layer caught a model regression when Layers 1 and 2 showed nothing wrong.

A probabilistic AI model can silently degrade — generating technically valid, running code that is measurably worse — without triggering a single infrastructure alert or HTTP 500. This is the core observability challenge for Make, and it requires a third layer of monitoring that traditional platforms never needed.

Traditional observability answers three questions: Is the system **up**? Is it **fast**? Is it returning **correct** responses? These questions assume the system is deterministic — given the same input, it produces the same output. If it stops doing that, it throws an error. You can catch errors. You can page on errors.

AI platforms break this assumption. A model can produce valid, syntactically correct, fully running code that is subtly worse than what it would have produced last week. No exception is raised. No HTTP 500 is returned. The preview loads fine. The code runs. The user just gets a result that's 20% less coherent, 30% more verbose, or missing an accessibility attribute. That's **silent degradation**, and traditional observability is blind to it.

This is the core observability challenge for Make.

**Three layers of observability for an AI platform:**

**Layer 1 — Infrastructure.** CPU utilization, memory pressure, network latency, container restarts, disk I/O. This is standard SRE territory. Necessary but not sufficient. A healthy infrastructure can host a degraded model.

**Layer 2 — Application.** API success rates, preview load times, build pass rates, deployment success rates, session error rates. This layer tells you whether Make's *services* are working. Still insufficient — your services can be functioning while the AI output is trending toward mediocrity.

**Layer 3 — AI-Specific.** Model response quality, agent session completion rates, token usage trends, first-turn success rates, user satisfaction signals. This is where AI observability diverges from traditional platform observability.

blocks.question: A healthy infrastructure can host a degraded model — so what failure mode does Layer 1 observability miss entirely, and why?
blocks.answer: Layer 1 misses silent quality degradation: a model that generates technically valid but lower-quality code doesn't throw errors, consume unusual CPU, or produce HTTP 500s. Infrastructure metrics measure whether the system is running — not whether it's producing good outputs. A model that consistently generates slightly worse code is invisible to Layer 1 because the system is, technically, operating correctly.

blocks.content:
The challenge at Layer 3 is that quality isn't a single number. "Model response quality" doesn't emit a metric automatically. You have to construct proxy metrics that correlate with quality:

- **Build pass rate on first agent attempt** — if the model is generating better code, users need fewer "fix this" follow-up turns
- **Session abandonment rate** — users who close a session without deploying or exporting may be expressing dissatisfaction with the output
- **Retry rate** — users who hit "regenerate" are signaling the first output didn't meet their needs
- **User satisfaction ratings** — explicit thumbs up/down signals attached to agent outputs
- **Eval suite pass rate** — if you run a regression eval suite on every model update, you can catch quality regressions before they reach users (covered in depth in the Evals module)

For Make specifically, the observability stack should cover:
- **Preview health:** what percentage of previews load successfully, in under 3 seconds, without console errors?
- **Agent session outcomes:** of sessions that start, what percentage result in a working, deployed app?
- **Build pipeline:** what percentage of builds pass on the first attempt? What's the distribution of failure reasons?
- **Deployment success:** of projects the user attempts to deploy, what percentage go live without manual intervention?
- **Token efficiency:** are agent sessions consuming more tokens over time for equivalent tasks? (Leading indicator of prompt drift or context bloat)

## How to Think About This

The silent degradation problem is why Layer 3 is not optional — it's the only layer that can detect quality changes that don't produce errors. Think of it this way: Layers 1 and 2 are smoke detectors; Layer 3 is a carbon monoxide detector. Carbon monoxide is colorless and odorless — it poisons without the alarm going off. A model that consistently generates slightly worse code is invisible to Layers 1 and 2 for the same reason. Proxy metrics like build pass rate and retry rate are your carbon monoxide sensor.

The silent degradation problem makes the Platform PM's job harder than traditional infra work. You can't just watch the error dashboard. You need to build a quality signal architecture — a set of proxy metrics that together triangulate whether the model is actually helping users accomplish their goals, not just technically running.

---

blocks.question:
A new version of Claude is deployed to Make's agent infrastructure. Your traditional monitoring shows: preview success rate 99.1% (normal), p95 preview load time 2.8s (normal), API error rate 0.2% (normal). But 72 hours later, user satisfaction ratings drop from 4.2 to 3.6 out of 5.

Which observability layer caught this, and which layers missed it?
blocks.answer:
Layers 1 and 2 missed it entirely — infrastructure and application metrics were nominal. Layer 3 caught it, via the user satisfaction signal. This is why you need all three layers. The model regressed; the infrastructure didn't.

---

blocks.title:
Open Figma Make and start an agent session. Build something with at least 3 components — a form, a card, and a navigation bar. Count the number of turns you take before you get a result you'd deploy. Note every time you hit "regenerate" or write a correction prompt.

Now imagine you could see those counts aggregated across 50,000 users per day. Which patterns would signal model quality problems? Which patterns would signal UX problems in the interface (not the model)?

*The distinction matters: if retry rate spikes after a model update, it's the model. If it spikes after a UI change, it's the interface. Your observability needs to capture both dimensions so you can attribute correctly.*

---
blocks.solution: After your session, you should have a turn count and a list of correction or regeneration prompts. A well-functioning model typically produces a deployable result in 2-4 turns for a 3-component build; if you needed 6+ turns or multiple regenerations, that's a signal worth noting. When you imagine this at scale: a spike in retry rate correlated with a model update strongly suggests model regression — the UX didn't change, so the retries are attributable to output quality. A spike after a UI redesign (new button placement, revised generation flow) is more likely a UX friction signal, even if the model is unchanged. The key diagnostic question is always: what changed between before and after the spike? If only the model version changed, the model is the suspect. If the interface changed, start there. Build pass rate on first attempt is your cleanest quality proxy because it's causally linked to output correctness, not user preference or interface confusion.

blocks.prompt:
Without looking at the lesson: explain the "silent degradation problem" to an engineer who's worked on traditional web services their whole career. Why can't they just watch the error rate? What do they need to add?

---

questions: Think of a product you've worked on or closely observed where quality degraded gradually — not a crash, but a slow erosion of how well it served users. What signals, if any, surfaced it? Looking back, what proxy metric would have caught it earlier?

When you've been responsible for a product with an AI or ML component — or worked alongside a team that was — what did Layer 3 observability look like in practice? Was there a dashboard, or did quality regressions show up in support tickets first?

Recall a time you were on the receiving end of a product that silently got worse. What was your behavioral response — did you retry, abandon, or complain? How would that behavior have shown up as a metric signal on the platform side?

---

blocks.content:
AI platform observability requires a third layer — AI-specific quality signals built from proxy metrics like build pass rate, session completion rate, and retry rate — because a probabilistic model can silently degrade without triggering any infrastructure or application alert.

---

blocks.prompt:
You know *what* to measure. Next: *how* do you actually capture it? The three pillars — logs, metrics, and traces — are the instrumentation primitives that make observability possible. How do they divide the work?

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is 'silent degradation' in the context of AI platform observability?
options: A model outage that occurs during low-traffic hours when monitoring dashboards are not actively watched
A gradual increase in infrastructure costs that doesn't trigger spending alerts
A model regression where quality worsens — generating technically valid, running code that is measurably less helpful — without triggering any infrastructure error or HTTP 500
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module defines silent degradation as the core observability challenge for AI platforms: a model can generate valid, syntactically correct, fully running code that is subtly worse than before. No exception is raised, no HTTP 500 returned, the preview loads fine — but the output is 20% less coherent or missing an accessibility attribute. Traditional observability is blind to this.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the definition — technically valid but measurably worse output, invisible to infrastructure and application monitoring.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Silent degradation isn't about timing or costs — it's about output quality. The model keeps working, the servers stay healthy, but the generated code gets worse. No alarm fires because the system is technically operating correctly. This is why Layer 3 AI-specific monitoring exists.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following is a Layer 3 (AI-specific) proxy metric for detecting model quality regression, as identified in the module?
options: Build pass rate on first agent attempt
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module explicitly identifies build pass rate on first agent attempt as a Layer 3 AI-specific metric: if the model generates better code, users need fewer 'fix this' follow-up turns. Container restart rate and API error rate are Layer 1 infrastructure and Layer 2 application signals respectively — they don't change when model quality silently degrades.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — build pass rate on first attempt is causally connected to model quality in a way that infrastructure metrics are not.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Container restart rate and API error rate are Layers 1 and 2 signals. A model generating subtly worse code doesn't produce more container restarts or API errors — it produces valid-looking output that users implicitly signal dissatisfaction with through retries and session abandonment. Build pass rate on first attempt is the Layer 3 signal.

reviewQuestions.question: In the module's calibration example, infrastructure metrics stay normal for 72 hours after a new Claude version deploys. What Layer 3 signal finally surfaces the regression?
options: Session error rate increases to 2.1%, crossing the alerting threshold
User satisfaction ratings drop from 4.2 to 3.6 out of 5
p99 preview load time increases from 2.8s to 4.1s
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's calibration check presents this exact scenario: preview success rate 99.1%, p95 load time 2.8s, API error rate 0.2% — all normal. Layers 1 and 2 miss the regression entirely. Layer 3 catches it through the user satisfaction rating drop from 4.2 to 3.6. This demonstrates why all three layers are necessary.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the example from the module — user satisfaction ratings are a Layer 3 signal that the infrastructure layers missed entirely.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: In the module's scenario, session error rates and load times stayed normal throughout — that's the point. The only signal was the user satisfaction rating drop from 4.2 to 3.6, a Layer 3 AI-specific metric. The model regressed; the infrastructure didn't.
